Summary of what was found: Trexel defines MuCell as microcellular foam injection molding using supercritical N2 or CO2 and reports part-weight reductions of 5%–40%, faster cycle times up to 40%, lower clamp force of 25%–50%, and energy savings of 5%–30%.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physical microcellular foaming for thermoplastics. Packaging/component relevance: injection-molded cosmetic parts such as caps, overcaps, compact shells, refill cases, trays, and opaque rigid components because the source describes lower density, reduced sink/warp, and a solid skin layer around the foamed core. Weight/material reduction evidence: explicit ranges for weight, cycle-time, clamp-force, and energy reduction. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: strong baseline for evaluating whether the same external part concept can be produced with less resin. Premium versus mass-market fit: stronger for mass-market or hidden/semivisible parts; premium use conditional because surface quality depends on application. Limitations/caution: process-level technology-provider evidence rather than a cosmetic brand case study.

Summary of what was found: Trexel says its consumer-packaging blow-molding solution can foam 2+ layer bottles and reduce bottle weight by up to 25% while maintaining similar top-load, impact, and ESCR performance; it also lists cosmetics as an application and can help offset higher PCR cost.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed blow molding for multilayer PE/polyolefin bottles. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic bottles and household/personal-care containers because the source explicitly lists cosmetics among typical applications. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 25% bottle-weight reduction at similar thickness/performance; example where a bottle foamed 15% and containing 35% PCR could be produced at the same cost as a virgin solid bottle. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: strongest for opaque or pigmented bottles where reduced density and PCR economics matter more than glass-like clarity. Premium versus mass-market fit: mainly mass-market; weaker for clear premium bottles because opacity is noted as a benefit. Limitations/caution: requires a multilayer machine and turnkey blow-molding setup with W. Müller; not plug-and-play for every bottle platform.
Source name: Trexel Brings Its MuCell Technology Beyond Automotive Applications — Trexel Inc. — https://trexel.com/trexel-brings-its-mucell-technology-beyond-automotive-applications/

Summary of what was found: PlasticsToday reports Borealis and Trexel developed a lightweight, fully recyclable bottle made with MuCell foaming; the foamed bottle keeps performance at virgin-like levels, lowers production energy demand, and can retain a premium look and feel.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physical foaming in blow-molded polyolefin bottles. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic bottles, refill outer shells, or premium-looking opaque rigid packs because the article highlights design freedom, color flexibility, and premium appearance. Weight/material reduction relevance: foaming reduces density and lowers energy consumption in production. Premium versus mass-market fit: important for premium/luxe screening because it suggests foamed polyolefin does not automatically force a low-grade appearance. Use in screening: keep certain foamed bottle or shell concepts in the premium-conditional shortlist rather than rejecting them outright. Limitations/caution: trade-publication article summarizing company statements; use as directional evidence rather than independent third-party validation.
Source name: Borealis, Trexel Develop Reusable, Fully Recyclable Lightweight Plastic Bottle — PlasticsToday — https://www.plasticstoday.com/packaging/borealis-trexel-develop-reusable-fully-recyclable-lightweight-plastic-bottle

Summary of what was found: StackTeck says microcellular molding for packaging offers 5%–20% part-weight savings, reduced clamp tonnage, better thin-to-thick flow, and reduced sink marks/warpage; it is being applied in rigid packaging, medical, and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: MuCell-type microcellular foaming in injection molding. Packaging/component relevance: personal-care closures, lids, tubs, jars, and thin-wall packaging because StackTeck is a packaging/personal-care mold supplier. Weight/material reduction evidence: explicit 5%–20% part-weight savings. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: strong evidence for near-term commercialization of foamed closures, lids, refill pods, and certain cosmetic jars/tubs without changing pack size. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market or standard personal-care packs; premium applications need appearance validation. Limitations/caution: supplier/process evidence showing feasibility and tooling logic, not a brand-side finished-pack case.

Summary of what was found: SABIC’s packaging page says polymeric foams can meet packaging weight-reduction needs while maintaining required properties, and lists foamable packaging applications including bottles, cups, corks, pouches, cap seals, food containers and packs across LDPE and PP foam families.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamable PP and PE packaging resin platforms. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic bottles, cap seals, closures, pouches, and rigid secondary parts where PP/PE remain preferred commodity materials. Weight/material reduction relevance: frames polymeric foams as a packaging route for reducing weight while preserving functional properties. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily mass-market because it centers on PP and PE packaging materials. Use in screening: evidence that foamable PP/PE resin grades are commercially available from a major material supplier, supporting scalability and supplier discussions. Limitations/caution: not a finished cosmetic case study or direct performance dataset for a specific beauty pack.

Summary of what was found: LyondellBasell says Polybatch chemical-foaming masterbatches can reduce plastic consumption by up to 20% on existing injection-molding or extrusion equipment, with packaging uses including caps and closures, containers, and thermoformed sheet and trays.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: chemical foaming for packaging. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic caps, closures, rigid trays, inserts, and secondary plastic components because the source lists these application families. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 20% plastic-use reduction, plus reduced sink marks, reduced warpage, and reduced cycle time. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: strong for mass-market programs needing low-capex retrofits and high-speed packaging conversion. Premium versus mass-market fit: better for mass and hidden components than high-gloss premium display parts. Limitations/caution: additive-based route; users must determine suitability and legality, so cosmetic-pack proposals need recyclability, migration, and regulatory confirmation.
Source name: Polybatch Chemical Foaming Masterbatches for Packaging Applications — LyondellBasell — https://www.lyondellbasell.com/globalassets/sites/lyb-documents/masterbatch/polybatch-chemical-foaming-masterbatches-for-packaging-applications.pdf

Summary of what was found: A LyondellBasell technical presentation says foamed parts can reduce weight by 5%–30% depending on technology, process, blowing-agent level, and article design; chemical blowing agents are often preferred to avoid expensive machine modifications, and food-packaging approval under EU legislation is referenced.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: chemical and physical foaming, including low-pressure and core-back routes. Packaging/component relevance: useful for choosing between simpler downgauge-style foaming and higher-savings foamed-core structures in rigid packaging parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: low-pressure process gives roughly 5%–10% reduction with wall stock maintained, while higher-savings/core-back routes can reach up to 30%. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports practical screening logic: start with lower-complexity chemical/masterbatch routes for caps, trays, inserts, and tubs; reserve more complex foamed-core routes for parts that justify extra process development. Limitations/caution: conference deck rather than peer-reviewed paper; food-approval context cannot be assumed to equal cosmetic compatibility.
Source name: How to make plastics lighter? Light weighting using Polybatch blowing agents — LyondellBasell — https://www.lyondellbasell.com/globalassets/sites/2023/mme-2023/session-3/how-to-make-plastics-lighter.pdf

Summary of what was found: ENGEL says thermoplastic foam injection molding creates a foamed core with a compact outer layer, allowing weight/material reduction while maintaining mechanical properties; it warns that escaping gases can create surface streaks and visible parts may need foilmelt, variomelt, or gas counter pressure for higher-quality surfaces.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physical or chemical foam injection molding with surface-upgrading options. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic overcaps, compact cases, refill shells, trays, collars, and non-contact rigid parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: foaming lowers weight and energy use, reduces machine loads, and can reduce rejects; a 30% material-savings example is shown on PCR building blocks. Premium versus mass-market fit: crucial screening source because visible Class-A surfaces are possible but may require extra surface technologies, changing cost and complexity. Use in screening: use foaming first on hidden or semivisible parts; treat glossy premium exteriors as conditional. Limitations/caution: process-provider evidence with many examples outside beauty.
Source name: Foaming of plastics in injection moulding — ENGEL — https://www.engelglobal.com/en/products/injection-moulding-processes/plastic-foaming

Summary of what was found: Huarong describes physical microcellular foaming as a clean alternative to chemical foaming and states typical weight reduction is 15%–20%, with 10%–20% often realistic; it also says Class-A/high-gloss surfaces generally favor solid molding or skin-core-skin approaches, and lists usable materials including PP, ABS, PC/ABS, PS, PBT, and some PA grades.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: microcellular physical foaming and comparison with chemical foaming. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic compact cases, overcaps, rigid shells, trays, and engineering-plastic parts where aesthetics matter. Weight/material reduction evidence: stated 15%–20% reduction without compromising structural strength, plus lower clamping force and cycle-time reduction. Premium versus mass-market fit: important caution source for premium/luxe because it explicitly flags high-gloss visible parts as difficult. Use in screening: textured, matte, satin, or decorated premium parts may be feasible; crystal-clear or mirror-polished premium parts should be treated cautiously. Limitations/caution: machine-supplier technical guide, practical but not cosmetic-brand-specific.
Source name: Microcellular Foam Injection Molding: A Complete Guide to Lightweight and Energy-Efficient Production — Huarong Group — https://www.huarong.com.tw/page/news/en/company_news/detail/114/

Summary of what was found: Matsui’s MuCell page says physical foam molding can achieve 20%+ material/weight reduction, leaves no chemical residues in the polymer, maintains regrind properties closer to virgin material, and is compatible with injection, extrusion, and blow molding; it also shows a 36 g to 29 g part example.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physical foaming with supercritical N2/CO2. Packaging/component relevance: rigid cosmetic packaging and packaging materials because the source explicitly mentions packaging materials and blow-molded containers among case studies. Weight/material reduction evidence: 20%+ reduction headline; explicit 36 g to 29 g example; up to ~30% reduction through foaming/material reduction in practice. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: strong for caps, trays, rigid cases, and some bottles where recyclability and internal regrind reuse matter. Premium versus mass-market fit: mostly mass-market, but applicable to semivisible premium shells if finish targets are modest. Limitations/caution: examples include automotive/industrial parts; cosmetic direct-contact suitability must be validated separately.
Source name: Physical foam molding MuCell — Matsui Mfg. Co., Ltd. — https://matsui.net/en/lineup/mucell/

Summary of what was found: RecyClass tested foamed PE liners on HDPE jars and lids often used for cosmetics and food; a 2024 revision concluded those foamed liners were fully compatible with HDPE recycling under assessed conditions, despite earlier concerns about blow-molding disruption.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed PE liner/seal elements attached to rigid HDPE packs. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic jars, closures, liners, and seals because the source says such packaging is often used for cosmetics. Weight/material reduction/route relevance: not a large primary-pack lightweighting route by itself, but liners, gaskets, and foamed sealing elements are common in beauty packs. Use in screening: do not automatically reject small foamed liner elements, but keep them separate from full foamed-container proposals. Recyclability implication: compatibility depends on assessed technology and conditions; route-specific testing matters. Limitations/caution: addresses foamed elements, not full foamed HDPE containers.
Source name: Foamed elements (liners, seals and valves) on HDPE containers Revised: 15 July 2024 — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Technical-Review-Foaming-Revised.pdf

Summary of what was found: RecyClass says its 2025 update extended the recommendation on PO foamed liners to the PP recycling stream and emphasizes that packaging recyclability depends on component behavior in specific sorting and recycling processes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: design-for-recycling status of foamed polyolefin liner elements. Packaging/component relevance: PP cosmetic closures, lids, and jars using foam-based sealing pieces. Testing/compliance implication: evaluation is based on standardized testing protocols and independent facilities, supporting D4R evidence rather than generic assumptions. Use in screening: small foam elements in PP closures can remain in scope if they align with current RecyClass guidance; larger or novel foamed attachments need case-specific review. Limitations/caution: addresses attached foamed elements, not full foamed PP containers.
Source name: RecyClass updates Recyclability Evaluation Protocols and Design for Recycling Guidelines with latest scientific findings — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/news/recyclass-updates-recyclability-evaluation-protocols-and-design-for-recycling-guidelines-with-latest-scientific-findings/

Summary of what was found: Gneuss says physically foamed PET sheet can be made from virgin PET and/or recycled PET, including 100% post-consumer material, with over 50% weight reduction depending on process while maintaining consistent foam structure and mechanical properties; target uses include thermoformed trays, cups, and pots.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed PET sheet extrusion and thermoforming. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic trays, inserts, thermoformed secondary components, sample pods, and protective secondary parts rather than clear premium bottles. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 50% weight reduction in sheet compared with rigid solid PET sheet. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: attractive for lightweight tray/insert routes where rPET content and reduced sheet mass are valuable. Premium versus mass-market fit: stronger for mass-market secondary packaging than luxe primary packs because foam sheet is not a transparent glass-like aesthetic route. Limitations/caution: opacity and thermoformed-sheet format limit use for visible premium jars/bottles.
Source name: From Rigid to Foam Sheet on the Fly: Fast and Flexible Production Changes with Gneuss Sheet Lines — Gneuss Kunststofftechnik GmbH — https://www.gneuss.com/en/from-rigid-to-foam-sheet-on-the-fly-fast-and-flexible-production-changes-with-gneuss-sheet-lines/

Summary of what was found: SML says premium foamed PP sheet can reach a density of 0.55 g/cm³ in an A/B/A structure, corresponding to 40% material reduction versus regular PP sheet; it is fully recyclable mono-material, fat/oil resistant, and suitable for thermoforming.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physically foamed PP sheet for thermoforming. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic trays, refill trays, secondary inserts, opaque tub/case components, and some tertiary protective parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: 40% reduction versus regular PP sheet, with a foamed B-layer made using inert gas and nucleating agent. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: good candidate for non-product-contact thermoformed components where the company wants lighter plastic while staying in PP. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market or hidden components; premium visible use requires aesthetic validation. Limitations/caution: food-packaging oriented, so formula-contact and beauty-decoration performance need verification.
Source name: High-performance PP sheet lines now equipped to manufacture premium foamed sheet — SML Maschinengesellschaft mbH — https://www.sml.at/news/high-performance-pp-sheet-lines-now-equipped-manufacture-premium-foamed-sheet

Summary of what was found: Bockatech says EcoCore’s skin-foam-skin PP packaging can reduce plastic density by about 70%, create recyclable mono-material PP packs, and deliver lighter, stronger, insulated packaging; the same solution can be used for caps/closures and not just cups.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: skin-foam-skin injected PP packaging using EcoCore. Packaging/component relevance: thicker rigid beauty parts such as refillable outer shells, robust overcaps, aerosol caps, and selected secondary parts because the source extends the concept to caps/closures and healthcare. Weight/material reduction evidence: around 70% density reduction in foam walls, lighter-than-some-paper-cup performance, and short cycle time. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and durable/reusable packs; also relevant to premium refill systems where tactile stiffness is needed. Compatibility/limitation notes: cited product is food-safe, but cosmetic direct-contact compatibility requires separate validation. Use in screening: keep skin-foam-skin PP in the longlist for overcaps, refill housings, and durable cases.

Summary of what was found: Arburg reports Bockatech EcoCore cups made with PP and combined chemical/physical foaming are up to 50% lighter than non-foamed alternatives and can be produced in 5.5 seconds cycle time.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: combined physical and chemical foaming in PP with skin-foam-skin structure. Packaging/component relevance: high-speed evidence for commercially realistic production of lightweight rigid packaging, relevant to overcaps, refill cups/pods, cases, and other thick-walled beauty parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 50% lighter than non-foamed alternatives; high-speed cycle supports scalability. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and durable/reusable packaging, but could extend to premium outer shells if surface and decoration targets are feasible. Limitations/caution: case is a cup, not a cosmetic pack; meaningful because it proves foamed rigid packaging can run fast and stably at industrial scale.

Summary of what was found: Milacron describes structural foam as producing parts with a solid skin and cellular foamed core, generating a high strength-to-weight ratio and enabling thicker wall sections at much lower pressure than conventional molding.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: structural foam injection molding for thick rigid plastic parts. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic secondary/tertiary parts and thick non-precision components such as merchandising trays, shipping inserts, refill caddies, rigid transport aids, or large outer shells. Weight/material reduction relevance: source frames the technology as a high-strength-to-weight solution for thicker parts but does not give a percentage on the webpage. Premium versus mass-market fit: more relevant to hidden or large-format components than small glossy closures or tight-tolerance dispensing parts. Limitations/caution: less attractive for precision threads, glossy overcaps, and small Class-A beauty components because optimized for thicker, larger parts.
Source name: L-Series: Low-Pressure Injection Molding Machines — Milacron — https://www.milacron.com/product/l-series/

Summary of what was found: World Aerosols reports Bockatech’s EcoCore technology can cut plastic use in overclosures by up to 44% while improving performance and reducing cost; the overclosures are made entirely from PP and can use recycled PP.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed PP overclosures/overcaps. Packaging/component relevance: direct beauty-adjacent source because aerosols and personal-care overclosures are close analogs to cosmetic overcaps. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 44% plastic reduction in overclosures. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong shortlist candidate for mass-market deodorant/body-care/aerosol caps and possibly fragrance or haircare overcaps; premium use conditional because flawless gloss, metallization, or luxury tactility may be challenging. Use in screening: keep foamed PP overcaps in the beauty shortlist rather than limiting foaming to trays and hidden parts. Limitations/caution: article is partially gated and summarizes company claims; useful as sector-specific directional evidence.
Source name: Bockatech’s EcoCore technology reshapes sustainable aerosol packaging — World Aerosols — https://worldaerosols.com/news/bockatechs-ecocore-technology-reshapes-sustainable-aerosol-packaging/
